levelMeaning: Does it independently on familiar problems.
Does it consistently and handles unfamiliar problems.
Elevates the work of others in this area.
Defines the standard for the organization.

skills.definition: How well you understand your squad's slice of GHN logistics: the operations, actors, and constraints behind the screens.
skills.summary: Do you understand how your part of the delivery operation actually works?
L: Knows core logistics terms but needs guidance on how the squad's area works.|understands what COD means but asks how the cash reconciliation flow works.
Works independently within the squad's area.|handles pickup-scheduling work without needing the operating rules re-explained.
Fluent in the area and connects it to nearby operations.|sees how a change to pickup windows affects hub sorting downstream.
Teaches the domain to others and catches flawed assumptions in review.|flags that a proposed flow ignores how rural zones batch deliveries.
Defines how the team builds and keeps its domain knowledge current.|sets up the shared reference the whole team uses to learn the operation.

skills.definition: Using metrics and instrumentation to understand how the product is performing, and backing design decisions with evidence rather than opinion.
skills.summary: Can you find, read, and act on the numbers that show whether the product is working?
L: Can read a dashboard when someone shows which numbers matter.|opens the driver-app funnel dashboard and reads the drop-off rate once a teammate points to it.
Tracks the core metrics of their own feature without being asked.|checks the failed-delivery retry rate every week for the feature they own.
Diagnoses why a metric moved and specifies the events needed to decide.|traces a fall in COD collection to a missing 'cash confirmed' event and writes the tracking spec to capture it.
Connects metrics others treat separately and raises the team's data literacy.|links rising pickup delays to a hub-sorting change and shows the team how to read the two together.
Sets how the whole team uses data to drive design.|defines the standard set of metrics every squad reviews before and after a launch.

skills.definition: Understanding the end-to-end operational service behind a feature: pickup, hub, last mile, failed delivery, and COD, not just the screens.
skills.summary: Can you see the whole delivery journey behind the screen you're designing?
L: Understands the screens but not the operational flow behind them.|designs the tracking page without knowing what triggers each status.
Can map the end-to-end service for their own feature.|draws the full path a parcel takes for the flow they own.
Designs for real operational edge cases.|handles failed delivery, re-attempt, and return-to-seller in one coherent flow.
Improves cross-team service blueprints and spots systemic gaps.|finds where a handoff between hub and last mile drops parcel status.
Defines how the team designs for the whole service, not just the UI.|makes service blueprinting a standard step before any flow is designed.

skills.definition: Judgment about what to build and why: framing opportunities, prioritizing, and tying design to business outcomes.
skills.summary: Do you know what's worth building, and can you say why?
L: Executes a defined scope with little framing of the problem.|builds the screens in the ticket without questioning the goal.
Frames the opportunity for their own feature with some guidance.|reframes a 'redesign the tracking page' task around reducing 'where is my parcel' tickets.
Prioritizes independently and ties design to business outcomes.|sequences the failed-delivery work to cut re-delivery cost first.
Shapes the squad's strategy and sharpens how others prioritize.|reshapes the roadmap around the highest-cost delivery failures.
Sets product-design strategy that influences the roadmap.|defines the experience bets the team commits to for the year.

skills.definition: The thinking craft behind an interface: the flows, structure, and interactions that let someone finish a task, accessible to everyone by default.
skills.summary: Can you design a flow people can actually complete, including people with disabilities?
L: Applies existing patterns with guidance.|reuses the standard address-entry pattern in the shipper portal when told which one to use.
Designs sound flows and structure for familiar problems on their own.|independently designs the flow for scheduling a pickup time.
Solves unfamiliar UX problems, with accessibility built in from the start.|designs a new failed-delivery resolution flow that also works with a screen reader and one hand on a phone.
Raises the quality of others' UX and sets the bar for interaction.|reviews a teammate's returns flow and reshapes it so drivers finish it in half the taps.
Defines the team's UX principles and how interaction quality is judged.|writes the interaction guidelines every GHN app screen is measured against.

skills.definition: The visual craft: layout, hierarchy, type, colour and contrast, and polish.
skills.summary: Does your work look clear, consistent, and finished?
L: Uses components, but visual consistency still needs review.|builds a screen that works but misuses spacing and the type scale.
Produces clean, on-system UI independently.|ships a dispatch screen that matches the design system without rework.
High visual craft; hierarchy and contrast pass every time.|a dense driver-app screen still reads clearly at a glance in sunlight.
Raises the team's visual bar and art-directs the work of others.|does a visual pass on a teammate's screen that lifts the whole flow.
Owns the visual language and its standard.|defines how GHN product screens should look and feel.

skills.definition: Consuming, extending, and governing the shared component and token system.
skills.summary: Can you use the system well, and help it grow without breaking it?
L: Uses components correctly with guidance.|picks the right button and input when shown where they live.
Uses the system well and contributes small patterns.|adds a missing empty-state variant back to the library.
Extends the system with sound token and component decisions.|designs a new status-chip component that fits the token scale.
Governs contributions and keeps the system coherent across teams.|reviews proposed components and rejects near-duplicates.
Defines design-system strategy and governance.|sets how the system is versioned, contributed to, and adopted.

skills.definition: UX writing and content design: clarity, voice, and error and empty states.
skills.summary: Is your wording clear, consistent, and helpful at the hard moments?
L: Writes basic UI copy that needs review.|labels a button 'Submit' where 'Schedule pickup' would be clearer.
Writes clear, consistent microcopy independently.|names the tracking statuses in plain words a shipper understands.
Strong voice and clarity across flows, including edge and error states.|writes a failed-delivery message that explains what happens next without blame.
Coaches others on UX writing and sets content patterns.|creates the error-message pattern the team reuses.
Defines the product's content and voice standard.|writes the voice-and-tone guide for all GHN products.

skills.definition: Choosing the right fidelity and building prototypes to test ideas, including in code where useful.
skills.summary: Can you make something testable at the right level of realism, quickly?
L: Builds simple click-throughs with help.|links a few frames to show a happy path.
Prototypes at the right fidelity to test a question independently.|builds a tappable pickup flow to test with drivers.
Builds fast, realistic prototypes, including coded ones when needed.|codes a prototype that pulls sample parcel data to feel real.
Raises the prototyping practice and mentors others on fidelity choices.|shows the team when a paper sketch beats a polished mockup.
Defines how the team prototypes and validates.|sets the team's approach to what gets prototyped and how before build.

skills.definition: Choosing and running the right kind of study to answer a question: picking the method, designing it, recruiting the right people, and facilitating well.
skills.summary: Can you pick the right way to learn from users and run it without biasing the result?
L: Helps on studies and follows a method someone else chose.|takes notes and helps recruit drivers for an interview a colleague designed.
Runs familiar studies and picks a fitting method for the question.|chooses an unmoderated test to check whether shippers understand a new label.
Designs rigorous studies and facilitates well even on unfamiliar topics.|designs field visits to observe how drivers handle failed deliveries in real conditions.
Improves how others design studies and sets method standards.|reviews a teammate's survey and removes leading questions before it ships.
Defines the research methodology the team uses.|establishes when the team runs discovery, evaluative, and continuous research, and how.

skills.definition: Turning raw data into trustworthy themes, respecting both qualitative and quantitative rigor.
skills.summary: Can you turn messy findings into conclusions you can trust?
L: Summarizes findings with guidance.|lists what interviewees said without grouping it.
Themes qualitative data independently.|groups driver interviews into a few clear pain themes.
Synthesizes rigorously and respects quantitative limits.|resists over-reading a 12-person survey and says so.
Coaches analytical rigor and catches weak inference in others' work.|points out that a correlation in the data isn't the cause claimed.
Sets the standard for evidence and synthesis.|defines how the team separates signal from noise in research.

skills.definition: Designing ongoing measurement of experience: CSAT, NPS, CES, survey triggers, and event specs.
skills.summary: Can you set up trustworthy ways to measure how the experience is doing?
L: Aware of CSAT and NPS but needs help to set them up.|knows the team runs CSAT but not how it is triggered.
Sets up basic surveys and tracking for their own work.|adds a short CSAT after the pickup-booking flow.
Designs sound measurement: triggers, timing, and event specs.|times a delivery-experience survey to fire after the parcel is received, not before.
Architects CX measurement across features and coaches others.|designs one consistent CX metric set across the shipper and driver apps.
Defines the CX-measurement framework for the team.|sets how GHN measures customer experience end to end.

skills.definition: Turning findings into decisions: recommendations that change what the team actually does.
skills.summary: Do your findings change what the team decides to do?
L: Reports observations without clear recommendations.|says users struggled, but not what to do about it.
Turns findings into actionable recommendations independently.|recommends a specific fix to the confusing tracking status.
Makes recommendations that drive decisions and tie insight to outcomes.|links a research finding to a roadmap change the squad accepts.
Elevates how others tell the insight-to-impact story.|helps a teammate reframe findings so leadership acts on them.
Defines how research influences product decisions.|builds the way research is brought into planning across the team.

skills.definition: How deeply AI is used in the craft, from everyday assistance to building automated, agent-based workflows, with judgment about output quality.
skills.summary: How much does AI multiply your work, and can you tell when its output is good enough?
L: Uses AI for basic daily tasks such as chat and drafting.|drafts UX copy variations for a tracking screen with an AI assistant.
Uses AI reliably across daily design work.|routinely uses AI to summarize support tickets before a design review.
Uses AI inside core craft and judges the quality of the output.|generates a coded prototype of the pickup flow with AI and catches where it invented a wrong state.
Builds workflows, uses agents and MCP, and coaches the team.|sets up an AI workflow that turns raw research notes into tagged themes and teaches the team to use it.
Architects AI-augmented workflows such as harnesses and evals for the team.|designs the team's system for checking AI output quality before it is trusted in production work.

skills.definition: Understanding feasibility and the stack well enough to design buildable work and collaborate with engineers.
skills.summary: Do you design things that can actually be built, and work well with engineers?
L: Aware that feasibility limits exist.|knows some things are 'hard' but not why.
Understands their own stack's constraints and hands off cleanly.|designs within what the driver app's framework supports.
Designs within constraints and collaborates strongly with engineers.|adjusts a flow when an engineer flags an API limit, keeping the intent.
Advises on feasibility trade-offs and improves handoff quality.|proposes a phased build that ships value before the hard part.
Sets the design-engineering collaboration standard.|defines how design and engineering hand off across the team.

skills.definition: Aligning people around the work: presenting, persuading with evidence, and handling disagreement.
skills.summary: Can you bring people along, and hold your ground with evidence?
L: Communicates within the team with guidance.|shares work in the squad channel when prompted.
Presents their own work clearly to peers and PMs.|walks the squad through a flow and the reasoning behind it.
Aligns cross-functional stakeholders using evidence.|uses failed-delivery data to win agreement from ops and product.
Influences leadership and coaches others' communication.|gets a director to back a direction, and helps a teammate do the same.
Sets how the team communicates and aligns.|defines how design reviews and decisions are run across the team.

skills.definition: Growing other designers through feedback, coaching, and sponsorship.
skills.summary: Do you actively make the designers around you better?
L: Shares knowledge informally when asked.|answers a teammate's Figma question.
Supports peers on familiar topics.|pairs with a junior on a flow they have done before.
Actively grows others and gives structured, specific feedback.|runs regular crit that visibly improves a teammate's work.
Develops multiple designers and visibly elevates their work.|coaches two designers whose craft levels up over a quarter.
Builds the team's growth and mentoring culture.|sets up the crit and feedback rituals the team runs.

skills.definition: Leading through reusable standards and patterns rather than through people, the IC form of leadership.
skills.summary: Do you make the whole team better by building standards, not by managing people?
L: Follows team standards.|uses the existing handoff checklist.
Applies standards consistently and suggests improvements.|proposes a small addition to the QA checklist.
Creates reusable standards for their own area.|writes the pattern for how delivery statuses are named and shown.
Drives adoption of standards across squads.|gets multiple squads to adopt one status-handling pattern.
Defines org-wide design standards.|owns the standards that shape how the whole team works.

skills.definition: Leading through people rather than through the work itself: managing designers, hiring, and owning their performance and growth. This is the management track and is not expected on the IC track.
skills.summary: Are you responsible for other people's performance, growth, and careers?
L: Early exposure, or not applicable on the IC track.|an IC designer who occasionally guides an intern informally.
Leads a small initiative or mentors an intern.|owns the onboarding of one new designer for a quarter.
Leads projects and coordinates people informally.|runs a cross-squad design initiative, coordinating three designers without being their manager.
Manages designers and owns their performance and growth.|holds regular one-on-ones, sets goals, and writes performance reviews for two designers.
Builds and leads the design organization.|shapes the team's headcount plan, hiring bar, and career framework.
